content.text: Class components express lifecycle logic through dedicated methods. Function components express the same logic through
— a single hook that unifies mount, update, and cleanup into one API. The mapping between the two models is approximate because their mental models differ fundamentally.

attrs.alt: Class lifecycle methods vs useEffect hooks — side-by-side mapping

content.text: Class lifecycle methods are imperative: you tell React which phase you care about.
is declarative: you describe a synchronisation concern and React decides when to run it.
runs once after the component's first DOM commit.
is the closest equivalent in function components.
runs asynchronously after the browser paint. Use
when you need synchronous post-mount behaviour.
. In function components you do not get previous values automatically — use a
to store the previous value if you need to compare.
The class version needs to compare
runs on every update. The dependency array in the hook does that comparison automatically.
The function component version makes it impossible to add the listener without also writing the removal — setup and cleanup are in the same block of code.
lets class components prevent re-renders by returning false.
provides the same capability for function components.
when props are equal (skip re-render). This is the OPPOSITE of
when the component SHOULD re-render — easy to confuse.
. In function components this pattern is unnecessary — derive values during render or use

The ref is updated inside the effect (after the render is committed) so that on the next render it holds the previous value. Updating the ref during render would make it hold the current value, defeating the purpose.
Class components access latest props and state via
— always current. Function component effects capture values from the render they ran in. If the dependency array is incomplete, the effect uses stale closed-over values.
If a value read inside an effect can change over time, either include it in the dependency array, or use the functional updater form of
to avoid reading it at all.
When to Still Use Class Components
New code should use function components and hooks. Class components remain appropriate for:
. Function-based error boundaries are not yet supported.
Migrating a large class component to hooks is often not worth the risk if the component is stable and well-tested.
Some older libraries provide HOCs or APIs designed for class components.

Class lifecycle methods map approximately to hook patterns: componentDidMount maps to useEffect(fn, []), componentDidUpdate maps to useEffect(fn, [deps]), componentWillUnmount maps to the cleanup return, shouldComponentUpdate maps to React.memo. Hooks describe synchronisation concerns rather than lifecycle moments. Stale closures replace stale-this — include all values an effect reads in the dependency array.
Here is the complete mapping of every class lifecycle method to its function component equivalent:
Runs before unmount or deps change
getSnapshotBeforeUpdate runs after render but synchronously before the DOM is updated, so you can capture scroll position or other layout data. In function components,
fires synchronously after every DOM mutation — you can pair it with a ref to read pre-commit values:
the commit (not before), so this pattern is slightly different. If you need the pre-commit read, capture it during render into a ref instead of reading it in the effect body.
Class components bundle all side-effects into a small set of lifecycle methods, forcing you to mix unrelated logic. With hooks you can have as many
calls as you like, each focused on one concern:

React runs each effect in declaration order. Cleanup for each effect runs independently when its deps change or the component unmounts. This is a significant maintainability improvement over class components, where you had to use if-blocks or private methods to separate concerns inside
fire synchronously after the browser paint.
runs asynchronously after paint — does not block the browser.
runs synchronously after DOM mutations, before paint — equivalent to the class timing.
as the default. Only reach for
when you can see a visual flicker with the async version.
The one lifecycle method still unavailable in function components is
. Error boundaries must be class components:
Wrap function component subtrees in a class error boundary. Libraries like
Testing hooks and effects is simpler than class components because you work with the component as a black box and React Testing Library handles the lifecycle automatically:
In class component tests you sometimes had to call
manually with Enzyme. With React Testing Library the lifecycle fires naturally during
, making tests more realistic and less coupled to implementation details.

Practice: take an existing class component that uses all three lifecycle methods and rewrite it as a function component using hooks. Compare the two side-by-side and identify where the function version collocates related logic and where the class version made timing more explicit.
